slides.title: Python Loves Numbers
slides.body: Numbers are everywhere in code — scores, prices, pixels. Python gives you **three built-in number types** so you always have the right tool for the job.
slides.tip: You don't need to import anything — int, float, and complex are built right in.

slides.title: Integers vs. Floats
slides.body: `int` is a whole number; `float` has a decimal point. Even `1.0` is a float — **the dot makes all the difference**.

slides.title: Three Kinds of Division
slides.body: `/` **always** returns a float. Use `//` for whole-number (floor) division, and `%` to grab the remainder.
slides.code:
a = 17
b = 5
print(a / b)   # float
print(a // b)  # int
print(a % b)   # remainder
slides.tip: 10 / 2 gives 5.0, not 5 — use // when you need a plain integer.

slides.title: The 0.1 + 0.2 Surprise
slides.body: Floats are stored in binary, so `0.1 + 0.2` isn't *exactly* `0.3`. Use **`round()`** when displaying decimal results to users.
slides.tip: This isn't a Python bug — every language that uses binary floating-point has this.

slides.title: Handy Tools: abs, round, math
slides.body: Python's built-ins `abs()` and `round()` handle everyday needs. For square roots, π, and more, import the **`math`** module.

slides.title: You've Got Numbers Down!
slides.body: You now know `int` vs `float`, all the arithmetic operators, the three flavours of division, and how to reach for `math` when you need more power.
slides.tip: Write big literals as 1_000_000 — Python ignores the underscores, but your eyes will thank you.
